method.population_source: U.S. Census Bureau Vintage 2025 incorporated-place estimates
method.selection: Next fifty largest incorporated U.S. cities above 100,000 population not already represented by BID Atlas or included in the first four large-city batches
method.active_hit_definition: At least one currently operating assessment-funded BID or locally named legal equivalent supported by an official source
method.notes: Candidate discovery and production boundary ingestion are performed together where an active district is verified. TIF-only, CRA-only, entertainment, residential, and capital-infrastructure districts are excluded. Cities already represented by a mapped district were skipped.

records.local_term: Proposed business improvement district (not formed)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded BID was verified in Murfreesboro. The City's comprehensive plan recommended establishing a downtown BID, but current FY2027 budget and ordinance materials do not show a formation ordinance, special levy, or district fund; the Central Business District remains a zoning and planning designation supported by direct City programs and Main Street activity.
records.production_next_action: Monitor the comprehensive-plan recommendation for a formation ordinance and first assessment; do not map the zoning district.

records.local_term: Property and Business Improvement District / Tourism Marketing District
records.summary: Oceanside has two active benefit-assessment districts. The Downtown Oceanside Property and Business Improvement District funds supplemental downtown improvements, maintenance, and activities, while the citywide Oceanside Tourism Marketing District assesses lodging and vacation-rental businesses for tourism marketing and sales promotion; the City renewed the OTMD in 2024 through 2033.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as generalized downtown and citywide selective-lodging benefit zones.

records.local_term: Property and Business Improvement District / Tourism Business Improvement District
records.summary: Lancaster has two active benefit-assessment districts. The Downtown Lancaster Property and Business Improvement District was renewed in 2023 and funds clean, safe, marketing, and downtown vitality work, while the Lancaster Tourism Business Improvement District was renewed for 2023-2033 and levies a 3% assessment on lodging businesses in its benefit zone.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as generalized downtown and selective-lodging benefit zones.

records.local_term: Community Improvement District
records.summary: Springfield has two active place-management CIDs that meet the atlas definition. The Downtown Springfield CID provides enhanced clean, safe, and welcoming services, and the Commercial Street CID—renewed in 2019 for fifteen years—supports the historic commercial corridor through district programs and a current 2026 operating budget. Developer CIDs focused on reimbursing project infrastructure are excluded.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as two generalized commercial service districts; exclude developer reimbursement CIDs.

records.local_term: Tourism Public Improvement District
records.summary: Denton's Tourism Public Improvement District is an active ten-year lodging assessment district formed in August 2024. The City currently collects a 2% assessment from seventeen hotels to fund targeted sales, marketing, public relations, and incentives for convention and group business; the FY2026 budget appropriates $1.203 million. A separate Downtown PID remains in outreach and survey stages and is excluded.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized selective-hotel benefit zone; monitor but do not map the proposed Downtown PID.

records.local_term: Property and Business Improvement District / Tourism Business Improvement Area
records.summary: Roseville has two active assessment-funded districts. The Downtown Roseville PBID supports marketing, promotion, improvements, and downtown business development, while the South Placer County Tourism Business Improvement Area assesses lodging in Roseville, Rocklin, and Lincoln and funds Placer Valley Tourism's destination and sports marketing programs.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as generalized downtown and Roseville selective-lodging benefit zones.

records.local_term: Tax Increment Reinvestment Zone / Main Street district (excluded)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded BID or commercial PID was verified in Killeen. Downtown revitalization is supported through Tax Increment Reinvestment Zone No. 2, City-funded grants, and the Texas Main Street program. The FY2026 adopted budget accounts for a TIRZ fund but does not identify a PID assessment or PID fund; tax-increment financing, historic overlays, and Main Street designations are outside the atlas definition.
records.production_next_action: Monitor for a PID formation petition or assessment ordinance; do not map the TIRZ, historic overlay, or Main Street district.

records.local_term: Tourism Improvement District
records.summary: The Palmdale Tourism Improvement District is an active lodging assessment district formed in October 2021. Effective November 1, 2021, the City collects a 2% assessment on taxable lodging receipts to fund tourism promotion and related services under California's Property and Business Improvement District Law of 1994.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized selective-lodging benefit zone within Palmdale.

records.local_term: Special Improvement District
records.summary: Paterson currently identifies the Downtown Paterson and Bunker Hill Special Improvement Districts as active assessment-funded place-management districts. Downtown provides clean, safe, marketing, and event services, while the Bunker Hill industrial district supports security, beautification, signage, and other supplemental amenities. Older code provisions for Sandy Hill and 21st Avenue were not treated as current without comparable operating evidence.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as two generalized commercial and industrial service-district boundaries; monitor other codified SIDs for current budgets before adding them.

records.local_term: Downtown Revitalization District / infrastructure assessment districts (excluded)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded BID or tourism district was verified in Corona. The City's current economic-development materials describe a City-led Downtown Revitalization Plan and specific-plan district, while its finance registry lists infrastructure, maintenance, Mello-Roos, and public-services districts rather than a commercial place-management assessment. Those planning and capital-finance districts fall outside the atlas definition.
records.production_next_action: Monitor downtown implementation for a PBID formation petition and management plan; do not map the specific plan or infrastructure assessment districts.

records.local_term: Proposed business improvement district (not formed)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded BID was verified in Alexandria. The City ended consideration of an Old Town BID in 2017, and its March 2026 FY2027 budget response still describes creating BIDs as a prospective effort costing an estimated $100,000 to $150,000 per typical district. A 2023 endorsement of an Old Town improvement-service concept did not establish an assessment district.
records.production_next_action: Monitor for City Council establishment, an approved district plan, and a first assessment; do not map the Old Town proposal.

records.local_term: Business Improvement District / Municipal Improvement District
records.summary: Charleston's King Street Business Improvement District is an active ten-year commercial assessment district created in 2022, with first assessment revenue realized in 2023. Eligible parcels fronting King Street from Broad Street to Line Street pay up to $0.0113 per dollar of assessed value for ambassadors, beautification, district management, marketing, and economic development administered by the Charleston Downtown Alliance.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized King Street corridor from Broad Street to Line Street.

records.local_term: Community Benefit District / private business improvement district
records.summary: The Salinas City Center Improvement Association manages an active property-owner-funded downtown business improvement district. The City identifies its boundary as the railroad tracks on the north, Monterey Street on the east, San Luis Street on the south, and Capitol Street on the west; assessments fund landscaping, cleaning, safety, marketing, and related district services.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as the generalized official downtown boundary stated by the City.

records.local_term: Self-Supported Municipal Improvement District
records.summary: Downtown Kansas City, Kansas has an active Self-Supported Municipal Improvement District established in 2008 and renewed for a ten-year period beginning in 2017. The property-tax district funds clean-and-safe ambassadors, security, maintenance, marketing, and downtown improvements administered with Downtown Shareholders; current 2026 assessor records continue to identify the SSMID taxing unit.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized downtown service-district footprint; verify the next renewal term when adopted.

records.local_term: Community Benefit District
records.summary: The Downtown Hayward Community Benefit District is an active property-assessment district formed in July 2018 for a ten-year term and administered by the Downtown Hayward Improvement Association. Its supplemental services include sidewalk cleaning, trash and debris removal, landscaping, banners, events, public relations, safety, and business attraction; the City Council received its latest annual activity report in March 2026.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized downtown assessment footprint; monitor the 2028 renewal.

records.local_term: Community Redevelopment Agency districts / business associations (excluded)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded commercial BID was verified in Hollywood. Downtown and Hollywood Beach receive place-management and redevelopment services through two Community Redevelopment Agency districts funded by tax increment, while the City's business-association registry describes membership organizations rather than compulsory benefit-assessment districts. The City's other current non-ad valorem districts concern neighborhood safety or infrastructure and are excluded.
records.production_next_action: Monitor for a commercial property-assessment ordinance or tourism-district plan; do not map CRA, voluntary association, or neighborhood-safety boundaries.

records.local_term: Business Improvement District
records.summary: Lakewood has two active commercial assessment districts. The Alameda Corridor Business Improvement District, established in 2003, funds beautification, economic development, and safety along Alameda Avenue, while the Lakewood-West Colfax Business Improvement District, formed in 2011, supports corridor identity, advocacy, business attraction, arts, and economic development; City Council approved its 2026 operating plan.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as two generalized commercial corridor assessment boundaries.

records.local_term: Business Improvement District
records.summary: The Downtown Sunnyvale Business Improvement District is an active business-based assessment district administered by the Sunnyvale Downtown Association. The City renewed the BID for FY2026/27 with roughly 271 businesses across three benefit zones and a projected $35,000 in assessment collections supporting marketing, events, visitor attraction, beautification, signage, and district administration.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized three-zone downtown business-assessment footprint.

records.local_term: Business Improvement District
records.summary: The Springfield Business Improvement District is an active downtown assessment district established in 1998. It supports business recruitment, events, placemaking, marketing, and clean-and-safe services; current City materials identify the BID as an economic-development partner, and the organization continues active 2026 programming.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized downtown assessment footprint.

records.local_term: Tourism Promotion Area
records.summary: Bellevue participates in the active Bellevue-Redmond Tourism Promotion Area, a lodging assessment district established to fund destination marketing, sales, tourism development, and visitor-economy programs. The joint advisory board recommends use of fees collected in the Bellevue and Redmond zones, and the City continues to approve annual TPA work plans and budgets.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized two-zone lodging-assessment footprint.

records.local_term: Special Service Area
records.summary: Naperville Special Service Area No. 36 is an active BID-equivalent property-tax district for downtown maintenance and marketing. City Council established it in October 2025 as the five-year successor to SSA No. 33; property owners fund one-third of enhanced maintenance and all of the Downtown Naperville Alliance marketing component.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized downtown special-service-area footprint.

records.local_term: Downtown Special Services District
records.summary: The Bridgeport Downtown Special Services District is an active property-tax-funded BID equivalent for the central business district. Its statutory purposes include preventing downtown deterioration and funding physical, economic, and social improvements; current 2026 City records document its ambassador, cleaning, beautification, marketing, public-art, and event services.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized downtown special-services-district footprint.

records.local_term: City Center Special Service Area
records.summary: The Joliet City Center Special Service Area is an active property-tax-funded BID equivalent supporting the Joliet City Center Partnership. The district funds downtown economic-development incentives, marketing, beautification, and business-climate programs; the City levied the SSA for 2025 and commissioned a 2026 study and redesignation process for its continuation.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized downtown special-service-area footprint; monitor the 2026 redesignation.

records.local_term: Public Improvement Districts (capital-development districts excluded)
records.summary: No operating place-management BID equivalent was verified in Mesquite. The City has four active PIDs, but their current service and assessment plans finance developer-installed infrastructure and related bond debt for residential or master-planned development. Iron Horse includes commercial uses, but its assessment likewise repays authorized capital improvements rather than ongoing district marketing, cleaning, security, or place-management services.
records.production_next_action: Monitor PID annual plans for a shift to ongoing enhanced services or formation of a downtown or tourism assessment district; do not map capital-debt PIDs.

records.local_term: Proposed downtown BID / Tres Lagos Public Improvement District (excluded)
records.summary: No operating commercial place-management BID was verified in McAllen. The City's downtown plan recommended creating a business improvement district, but current planning and redevelopment materials do not show that it was formed. The active Tres Lagos PID finances maintenance and services for a master-planned residential development and is not a downtown, tourism, or commercial district-management BID.
records.production_next_action: Monitor the 2026 downtown redevelopment process for a formation petition, ordinance, assessment plan, or tourism PID; do not map Tres Lagos.

records.local_term: Community Improvement Districts / proposed downtown BID (excluded)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded place-management BID was verified in Olathe. Older downtown design guidance proposed a BID for maintenance and organization, but the City's current downtown program relies on municipal grants, tax rebates, planning, and events. Olathe's active Community Improvement District mechanism reimburses eligible commercial development project costs through special sales taxes or assessments rather than funding an independent clean, safe, marketing, or district-management organization.
records.production_next_action: Monitor downtown implementation and CID plans for an ongoing place-management assessment; do not map project-reimbursement CIDs or the downtown zoning district.

records.local_term: Community Improvement District
records.summary: Savannah's Waterfront Community Improvement District is an active self-taxing commercial district created in 2023 and the first CID in Chatham County. Waterfront property owners pay an additional annual assessment for programs and projects above baseline City services; current work includes River Street planning, public-realm improvements, economic vitality, and implementation partnerships with the City.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized waterfront commercial-assessment footprint.

records.local_term: Proposed Business Improvement District / City-funded place management (not formed)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded BID was verified in Gainesville. The City commissioned a feasibility study, revenue model, and draft ordinance for a potential Downtown and Innovation District BID, but current 2026 materials still describe forming a place-management organization as an implementation goal. Existing ambassadors, cleaning, grants, events, and beautification are funded and managed through the Gainesville Community Reinvestment Area rather than a property or business assessment district.
records.production_next_action: Monitor for City Commission adoption of a BID ordinance, assessment roll, and independent management organization; do not map the GCRA or Sip & Stroll district.

records.local_term: No verified commercial PID, management district, or tourism PID
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded commercial, downtown, or tourism improvement district was verified in Pasadena, Texas. Current City financial, capital, economic-development, and planning materials show municipal programs, development zones, and citywide capital work but no Chapter 372 PID service plan, hotel assessment, commercial management-district levy, or BID organization.
records.production_next_action: Monitor City Council agendas and Chapter 372 petitions for a commercial or hotel-assessment district; distinguish Pasadena, Texas from Pasadena, California's multiple BIDs.

records.local_term: Former City of Palm Bay Business Improvement District (dissolved)
records.summary: Palm Bay created a dependent Business Improvement District in 2018 for corridor planning and economic development, but it never obtained a funding mechanism and was dissolved by Ordinance 2021-80. City staff confirmed in a 2024 workshop that the BID had been disbanded. Current economic-development grants and the former Bayfront CRA are City-funded or tax-increment mechanisms, not an active assessment BID.
records.production_next_action: Do not map the dissolved BID, former CRA, or residential CDDs; monitor for a newly funded commercial assessment district.

records.local_term: Public Improvement District / Tourism Public Improvement District
records.summary: Waco has two active BID-equivalent assessment districts. Public Improvement District No. 1, authorized in 2012 for fifteen years, funds enhanced downtown design, service delivery, marketing, merchant support, and organization. The Waco Tourism Public Improvement District, created in 2020, assesses 2% on overnight stays at hotels with at least 75 rooms to fund tourism marketing and hotel demand generation; a 2026 assessment-plan hearing is scheduled.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as generalized downtown and citywide lodging-assessment footprints; monitor PID1 renewal before its 2027 expiration.

records.local_term: RII-DII Business Improvement District (capital-finance district excluded)
records.summary: Thornton's RII-DII Business Improvement District is legally active, and the City approved its 2026 operating plan and budget. The City's financial report describes the approximately 75-acre district at 148th Avenue and Washington Street as a separate entity organized to construct and finance public improvements. No assessment-funded cleaning, safety, marketing, events, business support, or ongoing place-management program was verified, so this developer infrastructure-finance district is excluded under the Atlas definition.
records.production_next_action: Do not map the RII-DII BID unless a future operating plan documents ongoing assessment-funded place-management services beyond public-improvement construction, financing, administration, and debt service.

records.local_term: Downtown Management District
records.summary: The Downtown Midland Management District is an active Texas management district created in 2003 and funded by assessments on benefited downtown property. Its service plan funds development and value enhancement, urban design and beautification, marketing and image, and district administration. Current City materials continue to identify the district as an operating downtown partner, including a seat on the Midland Downtown Park Conservancy board.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as a generalized downtown assessment-district boundary; seek and substitute a current service and assessment plan if the district publishes one.

records.local_term: Proposed Special Service Areas / City-funded business district associations (not formed)
records.summary: No operating commercial Special Service Area or assessment-funded BID was verified in Rockford. City corridor plans describe an SSA as a possible implementation mechanism, while current downtown and business-district programs rely on municipal investment, TIF, grants, and City seed funding for voluntary business district associations. Current City materials do not identify an adopted commercial SSA levy, assessment roll, or designated assessment-funded service provider.
records.production_next_action: Monitor City Council ordinances for formation of a commercial SSA and a property-tax levy; do not map TIF districts, planning corridors, or voluntary associations receiving City grants.

records.local_term: Business Improvement District
records.summary: Columbia has two active property-assessment BIDs. The City Center Business Improvement District funds expanded downtown services and capital investments through City Center Partnership and runs through 2031. The City adopted the adjacent Vista Business Improvement District on June 9, 2026, with an assessment-funded improvement plan and a term through December 31, 2036.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as generalized City Center and Vista ordinance boundaries; monitor the first Vista management contract, work plan, and annual assessment roll.

records.local_term: Property and Business Improvement District / Business Improvement Area / Tourism Marketing District
records.summary: Visalia has three active BID-equivalent assessment programs. The Downtown Visalia Property and Business Improvement District, created in 1998 and renewed through 2029, funds downtown activities and improvements through the property owners association. The Town-Center Business Improvement Area adds a business-tax surcharge for promotion, events, decorations, and parking, with its management agreement renewed for 2026-27. The Visalia Tourism Marketing District assesses 2% of room revenue at lodging businesses with at least 20 rooms for destination marketing and sales through 2032.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as two generalized downtown benefit areas and a citywide lodging benefit zone; replace generalized geometry with current official GIS or parcel rolls when available.

records.local_term: Proposed downtown Property and Business Improvement District (not formed)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded BID was verified in Escondido. The City's strategic work plan schedules creation of a downtown Property and Business Improvement District as an FY2026-27 objective, and current economic-development materials describe assessment-district options rather than an adopted formation resolution, assessment ballot, management plan, or levy. Existing Community Facilities Districts finance development infrastructure and municipal services and are excluded.
records.production_next_action: Monitor FY2026-27 Council actions for a PBID management plan, petition, ballot, formation resolution, and first assessment; do not map the planning area, business enhancement zone, or developer CFDs.

records.local_term: Special Assessment District / Business Improvement District
records.summary: Syracuse operates two assessment-funded business districts. The Downtown Special Assessment District, established in 1975, charges downtown properties an annual fee for Downtown Committee programs supporting maintenance, beautification, marketing, business development, and district vitality. The Crouse-Marshall Special Assessment District, established in 2001, funds infrastructure maintenance plus management, marketing, and security. The City held FY2026-27 budget hearings for both districts in April 2026.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as generalized downtown and Crouse-Marshall assessment boundaries; replace with parcel-derived boundaries from the City's current property lists when practical.

records.local_term: Proposed downtown Business Improvement District / Urban Renewal District (excluded)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded BID was verified in Meridian. In June 2026 the City and downtown businesses were still exploring a BID and had not completed the property-owner petition or City Council formation process. The existing Downtown Urban Renewal District uses tax-increment financing for redevelopment and sunsets in 2026; it is not a BID assessment or independent place-management levy.
records.production_next_action: Monitor Council agendas for a signed BID petition, establishing ordinance, assessment formula, and service plan; do not map the Downtown Urban Renewal District or Destination Downtown planning area.

records.local_term: No verified commercial or tourism assessment district
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded commercial, downtown, or tourism improvement district was verified in Miramar. The City's similarly abbreviated BID program means Business Industry Development and supports procurement access for local and minority-owned firms; it is not a geographic assessment district. Current City financial, business-tax, planning, and economic-development materials do not identify a BID levy or independent place-management organization.
records.production_next_action: Monitor City Commission agendas for a geographic non-ad-valorem commercial assessment or hotel marketing district; do not confuse the Business Industry Development procurement program with a business improvement district.

records.local_term: Special Improvement District / Tourism Improvement District
records.summary: Elizabeth has three active assessment-funded districts. The Elizabeth Avenue Special Improvement District funds business recruitment, promotion, safety, security, and administration, with a $574,970 2027 budget. The Historic Midtown Elizabeth Special Improvement District's 2026 levy funds safe-and-clean services, marketing, and district operations. The Greater Elizabeth Tourism Improvement District collects hotel assessment fees for destination marketing; City Council approved nearly $1.93 million for its 2026 program.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as generalized Elizabeth Avenue, Midtown, and citywide lodging benefit zones; replace with official SID parcel boundaries and the current tourism assessment plan when available.

records.local_term: Suggested Old Town Property and Business Improvement District (not formed)
records.summary: No operating commercial or tourism BID was verified in Victorville. The Old Town implementation plan identifies a PBID as a possible future funding mechanism, but current revitalization programs use City capital projects, CDBG-funded facade loans, and a Community Revitalization Investment Authority rather than a property or business assessment for place management. The City's landscape, drainage, and maintenance assessment districts serve development and neighborhood infrastructure and are excluded.
records.production_next_action: Monitor Old Town implementation for a PBID petition, management plan, ballot, and levy; do not map the CRIA, specific-plan area, or infrastructure maintenance districts.

records.local_term: Development public improvement districts (capital-finance districts excluded)
records.summary: No operating assessment-funded place-management or tourism district was verified in Round Rock. The City's PIDs—including Behrens Ranch, Southeast, La Frontera, and the proposed financing for The District—reimburse developer-built water, wastewater, roads, drainage, utilities, or other public infrastructure. They do not fund ongoing cleaning, safety, marketing, events, business support, or an independent district-management organization.
records.production_next_action: Monitor for a downtown services PID or hotel-assessment tourism PID; do not map development reimbursement, infrastructure, utility, or residential PIDs.

records.local_term: Business Improvement District
records.summary: Jackson has two active commercial-property BIDs. The 65-block Downtown Jackson Business Improvement District assesses taxable commercial property at ten cents per square foot and funds Downtown Jackson Partners' clean-and-safe, marketing, business recruitment, and development work. The Fondren Business Improvement District assesses commercial property at eight cents per square foot for beautification, security, and economic development; City Council established its 2026 assessment in June.
records.production_next_action: Production-ingested as generalized downtown and Fondren business district boundaries; substitute the official GIS geometry for Fondren and parcel-derived downtown geometry in a future precision pass.
